Welcome to Getting Started, Lessons with the Masters. In this private conversation with John Carlton, you'll discover the most closely guarded secrets of the man considered by many to be the most ripped-off copywriter alive. Over the last two decades, John's promotions have continually allowed his clients to dominate their niche markets and make fortunes in the process. As a student, pay special attention to the powerful mindset John operates from. This mindset is a huge part of his success and the unbelievable results he's helped so many others experience. For more on John, visit him at www.marketingrebel.com. Now, let's listen in. Hi John. Hi Clayton. Sorry if I'm late. It took a while to get through the rigmarole there on the phone line. Sorry about that. I'm constantly looking for a good places to do this kind of stuff. Is this call being recorded to be transcribed or are there people on the line or what's going on? Nope. It's just you and me. Great. It's been recommended that Ben Savanga took a month and a half to get back to me. I was reading his and I thought boy he's eloquent. He's just got a brilliance for detail. That's Gary. How come you missed his seminar? I had some family stuff coming up at that time and I just couldn't get away. But you know Carleen went and several of my other copycups and man the response was just fantastic. Yeah it was also like old home week. I mean I saw people I hadn't seen in years. I know. I took more notes at that seminar. It was amazing. I earned my money back within the first 15 minutes. You know I cut my teeth on Gary Ben Savanga pieces. I used to go into John Finn's office to the big bin he had there. This is in the 84-85 and he just throw all his all the all the mail he got would go into this big bin and he reached in one day and he pulled one out. He says this is Gary Ben Savanga's. I can guarantee you it's a control so you might want to look at it closer than you would when you didn't know. You might not know it's a control. I went home and tore it apart and just shook my head and just thought this stuff is brilliant. I wound up stocking his pieces for a couple years after that. Just making lists of his verbs and his twists of phrase and things like that. It was very very important to my career. He was amazing. I really think that's a key though to growing. Everyone has all of these courses and books nowadays and there's something about techniques and strategies that you divine by reading somebody else's work as opposed to having concepts handed to you on a silver platter. That's right. And so many of the people I know, so many of the people that I know have made it, you know, good six and seven figure copywriters today, just never had a matter. They just they were hungry enough to really study the stuff that was out there. I know that's what I did. Yeah. And what's interesting about that is, you know, I think people get scared. In fact, what part of it, what I teach is how to rip effectively, how to take good pieces and rip them in the right way, not copying them. But I think sometimes people are afraid they're just going to be a clone of a Halbert or Carlton or a make piece or something. And that's not true. I never became a clone of Ben Zavinga, but through that door that he opened for me, I found my own voice, my own style. And, you know, it was tempered by Halbert and Abraham and all these other guys. So sure, there's no there's no danger of being a complete clone of anyone you study. It's it's it's a total myth is there's no way that two personalities can match up that that tightly. So oh, I agree. I agree. My stuff doesn't sound anything like Ben Zavinga's. But I studied him so carefully coming up. And in fact, I even hired him back in 1982. Oh, really? I had a exclusive arrangement with a coin company up in Minnesota. And we were doing really well. We sold about 120,000 subscriptions in a year to the newsletter, the money advocate, and then mathematics. Yeah. Yeah, I think I remember that. Yeah. And I wanted to see, you know, if somebody else could come in and and get us an incremental response, you know, increase in response. And so I hired Gary to come in and do a package for me. It was great. I don't remember the result at this point, but it was a great experience. I got to work with him then. Was he was he was he cheap at that point? No. Oh, he was he was excited. Gary was never cheap. Yeah. Well, you know, I remember learning all about royalties from him, him and Jim Rutz. Jim Rutz learned it from him, I believe. I actually don't know that to be true. That was my imagination because I was ghostwriting for Jim Rutz at the time when I first started finding out about royalties and commissions. Um, was that was after Jim's infomad days, right? Infomad? Yeah, I know nothing about that. Yeah, Jim was it. Jim followed me at Infomad after I left there. Oh, uh, Jim came in and did their copy for a while. And that's where he met Ed Elliott. And then the two of them went away. I gave Ed his first job out of college. Oh, really? And Jim and Ed then invented the magologue together. Yeah, I had dinner with Jim in New York. That was an enlightening experience. You know, he wasn't working. He was working with a couple of different designers. John Finn hooked me up with, uh, with Rutz. Uh, I was like John, you know, ace in the hole for a while. I was working for Phillips and a Goren. Remember Lee Euler? Sure. He's still around. He's writing good packages for a Gora. That's what that's what I heard. I thought he just disappeared. And then I found out to, you know, but he ran a tight little company back then. But anyway, John would hook me up with other writers. This was an experiment. He has, I think he's still doing it. And I did all the writing and I worked with a couple of guys, but Rutz was the main guy. And boy, was he hard to work for. And, uh, but it was worth it. I mean, he's just, it was just, you know, it was, it was like, uh, I don't know. It was, it was like building one of the Shiopp's pyramids. But at the end, you know, the piece just was solid as, as a rock and, and mailed for a long, long time for a remnant review. Um, and I only worked with him a couple of times and then he really hit it big with a piece with a Gora with Jim Elliott. It wasn't a bagel lock, but it was the first time he used major photography on the outside of the page. It was about the coming depression or something. I forget. And they just, and, and it was the outside of the package that, that really did it for him. So yeah, so often that's case. Um, he, I don't know. I went to, uh, Jim was it, I only met Jim once. It was at, at Elliott's wedding reception. Oh, and, uh, it was a lot of fun. I talked to him just the other day, uh, about doing one of these interviews and he's a little reluctant because he doesn't feel our subscribers really want to hear what he has to say because so much what he's, what he does is, is, uh, kind of spiritually grounded. Well, he is, I was trying to think of at the Algonquin table, you know, that, that was my big dream always was, you know, to get into writing was to just, you know, I really missed, missed that Algonquin table of 30, you know, Dorothy Parker and all the other people in New York. And I tried to think of who the Algonquin table would be among us, among the top, you know, direct male writers. And it's like, it's like the personalities are there. And I was trying to think of who Jim might be. And he was definitely off the charts on every, every, excuse me, every personality scale that I know of. Yeah. I, I, I have a degree in psychology. Now, Halbert is also off the scale, but, but in a much different way. I mean, you put these two in a room together and, you know, I'm not sure what, what would happen. It wouldn't be good. But, you know, it's just, it's just how wacky everybody was. And that was one of the appeals for me of, of high end freelancing was just the, the loosening of the bonds of social restraint, you know, of, you know, you could, you really could live your life as, as free as you wanted. So. Yeah, I don't think it's any coincidence. There's so many of the top writers are libertarians. You know, that I, I know, I know many of them are. And I am, although I belong, I don't belong to any organization. I used to be a, what was called a liberal libertarian. Do you remember when those still existed? No. Socially liberal, conservative, financially conservative. And there was a, there was a huge wing at the libertarian party. And it was just, it was more like, I think I got into it through the First Amendment being a First Amendment freak. And, you know, just, I was entranced by the free market and the whole idea of freedom. But I didn't, you know, but, but I kind of cut ties with, with a lot of the other libertarians on the, on the social issues side. So. Yeah. And then they, they, they all went away. There used to be a lot of them in the early 80s. It was amazing. There was a, in fact, they were going to run a separate candidate once. I think they became the truth and reason party, if, if I remember correctly. Anyway, that's. Yeah, that was one of the things about going to one of their conventions that Pro-Avortion booth would be right next to the Pro-Life booth. The gun control booth would be right next to the Second Amendment booth and a bunch of crazy people. Oh, sure. This is going to dispatch the first few questions of the interview right away. Sure. One of the things I wanted to draw you out on though a little bit is you've worked with, I don't know if you're aware, but, you know, Gary got started after he left Ogilvy with Dan Rosenthal. Right. And I think he was doing with Dan, which you were doing with Jay and Gary Halbert. And he credits that experience with Dan, although, like you said, it was pretty painful and it left scars. But he credits that experience with, with really accelerating his abilities as a copywriter. Right. Tell me a little bit about what you learned from Jay. Sure. Yeah, it's, it's, Gary at his seminar actually expanded on, on that and told a very, very long story about working with Dan. That was just fascinating and really, you know, and for me, it had had special meaning because I had gone through much of the same stuff. And like you, like you said, it left a mark, you know, I found out about Jay in a weird way. I was working with agencies in Los Angeles. And this is, you know, Los Angeles for a brief period with a shining example of what classic direct response could have been. All of the large Madison Avenue, New York agencies opened direct response agencies in Los Angeles. I think, I think there were some printing reasons. There was a tax reasons or something. I'm not sure, but, but O&M had one. There was just a lot of them. They only lasted for a few years and it was during the years that I came out as a freelancer. So I was getting work at these major agencies. And, and I was actually going to the creative guild out there and, and my path was looked like it was leading towards the direct marketing association, you know, that kind of classic kind of stuff. And I wasn't very happy because I was always running up against brick walls, essentially. You know, people didn't, you know, people, my creative directors, when I go in as a freelancer at these agencies, had never read Claude Hopkins. And if they had heard of him, they were very dismissive of it. And they, and, and actually I remember being at a, at a creative guild meeting where they talked for a very, very long time about the need to have graphic artists control the pieces, the direct mail pieces that went out, that there was too much copy control, that copy, you know, that copy wasn't King, essentially. And I just, I was just slack jawed at that. And I just thought, I am, I am, I am a heathen among, among unbelievers here. And what happened was Jay was running ads in the Los Angeles Times to try to find some, some writers and, and he used codewords. Like I believe he put Claude Hopkins and things like that in there. And to me, this was like something I really wanted. Well, Jay, I think ran one of these ads. I happened to find out about it. I sent in some samples and a letter, and he sent me back a form letter saying, of, of, you know, what you need to do is read Claude Hopkins and stuff, you know, your stuff isn't quite up to par and blah, blah, blah. And it was a form letter. He had obviously not read my stuff and hadn't read anybody's as I found out later. He just decided he didn't want to look for writers anymore. And I got so mad, I found out where he lived. And it turned out he was really close to where I lived at the beach in LA. So I went to his office, ready to haul him out onto the tarmac and have a fight. I mean, I was, I was very, very angry at the time because, you know, this was something I held near and dear with this whole idea of bringing classic advertising and classic salesmanship into the modern world and not letting it be lost. At this time, Claude Hopkins was out of print. The call your letter book was, was out of print. You know, these people weren't reading these things. It was a very, it was a dark ages for the classic side of advertising, even as I said, it was a, it was a, you know, a lot of companies that were getting into direct response that hadn't been into it before. And, you know, I, I wound up meeting, I didn't meet Jay that day. I met his, the guy who was running his office and he called me down and I wound up working for Jay. But what I did was I made the smartest move that I ever made. I didn't ask for money working with Jay. I did writing for him in exchange for free run at his office. And that meant I got to sit in on all the meetings that I wanted to that he had. I got access to all these back file cabinets and nobody had been into a while. And I found a bottom cabinet that was full of pieces by Ben Zvinga and Rutt that Jay had paid for in order to never mail. They were genius stuff. And, you know, through that, I met, I met John Finn, who became my agent for a while and just a lot of things opened up. And Jay, more than anybody else, I believe at that time, and this, this again was about in 1984, was just recognize the truth of the power of classic salesmanship translated into print as Claude Hopkins called it, salesmanship in print. And Jay was a salesman. He was a salesman at heart. He had done a lot of door to door sales. He had, he had been a salesman and he understood how to translate that into killer marketing campaigns. And he was at the time, he was just charging outrageous fees, $2,000 an hour unheard of. He was progressing towards a seminar where he was going to charge 5,000 and then very soon $15,000 to attend a seminar on marketing. This was at a time when the highest anybody had paid for marketing seminar was, I believe, $350 to go to a Joe Shurgaiman seminar at the time. And Jay just was fearless in going out there because his, his fearlessness came from the confidence that he had with the, with the background that he had in sales and that he knew that what he was talking about wasn't just theory, wasn't just something that could work. It was something that would work if applied correctly. And it just cemented everything that I had suspected and was trying to use in these agencies. Yet I wasn't able to get pieces out the way I wanted them because they would be edited by three layers of, you know, creative directors, lawyers, you know, and vice presidents of marketing. So Jay was just doing the direct thing with, with clients. It was like he would meet with the client. He would talk with them, tell them what he's going to do. He would actually write a lot of the stuff and then, and then the client would run it. So there was no interference. There were no layers of, of bureaucracy or censorship. And the results were just staggering at the time. So, you know, I couldn't have had a better education in the power of classic killer salesmanship. If you could draw one, one memorable lesson from what you learned working with Jay. What would it be? Oh, you're blindsided me with that because the thing that I remember most about Jay was the way he treated clients. When, when, when, when I became a freelancer, I decided that, you know, I needed to learn how to be a businessman. I read some business books. You know, as I said in the bio, I took a speed reading course and read everything I could in the library. And that was one of the best things I did. It kind of gave me an instant education in this. But the how to handle clients stuff of being a freelancer, as, as you probably know, most freelancers go out there thinking they, they, you're essentially a hooker off the street. You have to do what the client wants. You know, your job is to please the client. And what I learned from Jay was that the exact opposite was true. When you come in with the confidence that you know what's going on and you are the expert, you are the guy, you are the go-to guy, you're, you're not coming to this with a guess about what to do. And you're not coming like a painter who's going to paint whatever color the client wants on the wall. You're coming there as a person who's going to tell the client what he needs to do, how to do it. And then you're going to help him do it or, or do it for him if, if, if you wind up doing the writing. And that made me realize who was in charge and how to, how to position myself in the client, uh, uh, uh, freelancer relationship. And it changed, it changed everything. So rather than being the supplicant coming in and doing the bidding of the client, I started coming in and, and I, and I became fearless almost, almost immediately. And, uh, you know, essentially stopped being kicked around by clients because when you get kicked around by clients, uh, the stuff never goes out the way it should. And I used to, I remember thinking, God, he just ruined that headline by editing it. And I don't know if you ever had this earlier in your career claim, because I know that you too are fearless. But you know, the, the great indignity that I had was finding out that the client that took the piece that I had slaved over and then had his daughter, who was a freshman, uh, English major in college go over it and change all the language and change the headline and do everything so that it fit her idea of grammar and all this stuff. And it just killed the piece. And I didn't say anything. It's just, I said, okay, if that's what you want to put out, you know, you're the client, you're the boss. And after working with Jay, it was never again like that. It was, it was no, in fact, I started putting it into my contract that if he wanted to change the piece, the client could change it, but he had to mail it against the piece that I wrote as I wrote it. So, so the test would be there. And I was quite confident that I would demolish any kind of change he would do. And that's the way it's been through, through my, throughout my career. That's great. Yeah, Jay, Jay has always had that. I, my office was right next door to his in the 70s. I've been Palos Burdy's. That's when I first met Jay. And he's always had that attitude. And it's very healthy because it's, it's good for everybody, I think, because your clients get more winners. You get more winners. You make more money. So I mean, it's just a win-win all the way around. But, but as in a lot of other things in life, it isn't, it doesn't seem to be the way it doesn't seem to be common sense that the freelancer would come in, take money to do a job, and then tell the client and insist on doing it a way that makes the client uncomfortable. And that became actually a standard of mind. I wanted the client to be a little uncomfortable, mailing or printing or, or, or posting the pieces that I wrote. If he was comfortable with what I did, then I thought that I had, I took another look at what I wrote, because I wanted him to be a little nervous about it. Because if he wasn't nervous, that meant I hadn't taken him out of his comfort zone. And the reason he was coming to me, of course, was that his comfort zone wasn't cutting it. So I wanted him to be a little, a little uncomfortable about what was going to happen. Because edgy stuff, I, you know, I, I have found a micro edgy stuff works a hundred times better than, than, than safe stuff. Absolutely. Absolutely. I found the same thing. Whenever, whenever the client loved it, that's a red flag. That's great. Well, you went on and you, and, and you met with, you worked with Gary Halbert. In fact, I think I met you in Utah at one of Gary's information. It was in Utah or Florida. I actually remembered as being Florida down in Key West. But yeah, I actually met Gary at Jay's divorce party. A, an assistant to Jay, tried to cheer him up by having a big party and I went and I'd known about Halbert. And I met him and he was arrogant and dismissive and just that pompous ass. And, and, and I just liked him immediately. It's, it's, this was a strong personality. At, at the time I was starting to get, get a lot of work through the big mailers, as I call them, which is, you know, Phillips, Agourin and those guys. But there was, it still did, it just wasn't quite the kind of stuff that I, that fit my personality. That was what I wanted to do. I found it kind of a little too pedantic, pedantic for the transcriber. I found it a little stifling for me and Halbert was in this entrepreneurial world that to me was very exciting. Each job was completely new. Each person that came in brought a whole new set of, of guidelines of, of, paradigms of marketing angles to go after new markets, new, new, you know, whole new types of people we'd be going after with different kinds of things. And that to me just reinvigorated my love of what was going on because, you know, when things got too safe for me, I, I, I found it hard to get the edge that, that I wanted in, in my writing and Halbert made sure that the edge was always there. And that just, that just kept me writing at, at, at this real peak of potency where, you know, it's, it's just, you, I knew I couldn't relax. There, there was no net. We were working with, without a net, we were just going out and he was taking large sums of money at, at the time, just huge amounts of money from people and, and promising them that we would, you know, that, that, that, that, that that fee was merely a small investment in their future success. So we had to, you know, we took on financial responsibility for the survival of these entrepreneurial companies. And there was just great, you know, just a great feeling and being able to do that, being able to walk that arrogant line, although I never quite adopted the, the total, you know, blind confidence that, that, that Gary had. But just, just an idea of just being able to walk, walk into, walk into a place and just tell the client what's going to happen and, and, and how we're going to do it and, and then go do it. It was just, there's, there's no other feeling like it, I think in, in, in business. And when, when you met us in Utah, we were dealing with, I believe those roughs people, if it was Utah, otherwise it was a seminar down in, down in Florida, which we were charging a lot of money to have people come down to have us just kind of rip them a new one. And that's where I learned, by the way, that I, I have a reputation claim, Clayton, I don't know if you know this is giving the most brutal critiques in, in, in the biz. And that's part of the thing that I offer in my Insiders Club is people send me their material and I rip it apart. And, and the reason I'm brutal and the reason I have this rep is because I treat their stuff the way I treat my own stuff. And that, and, and that's, and, and I treat, and I'm very brutal on my own stuff because there's money on the line. This isn't writing for, for a grade at, at, at, you know, in, in school. This isn't writing for getting a, a, a pat on the back, you know, of, from, from somebody. This is, you know, if this stuff doesn't sell, it doesn't matter how, how nice it reads or how exciting it is. The only thing that matters is, is how it sells. And I learned that brutality from Halbert, the very first piece I wrote for him. I, as, as, as I said, I was already an established writer with the big mailers. He threw it back across the desk at me and said, it said, it said, go redo this and don't bring it back until you put a little god damned empathy into it. And I was just shocked. And I thought, is this not going to work out? Am I, you know, am I, is this, is this it? Am I going to have to jump across the desk or, you know, and strangle this guy or is he going to strangle me or what's going to go on? But then I gathered myself and I thought, no, I did this. I, I, I actually, you know, tried to arrange to work with Halbert for a reason. This is, you know, I'm in this to learn. I'm not my, you know, I've got, I had to leave my ego at the door. That, that, by the way, was a huge lesson I learned to leave my ego at the door. Nothing, you know, what counted was a purpose beyond my ego, which was making the piece work. And I went back and I did 17 versions of that first letter for Gary. It happened to be a diet piece for a client who was selling some diet stuff. And the 16th version was the worst of the lot. And I remember winding up, trashing it. And the 17th version that I finally had that breakthrough on, mailed for several years. And with the first royalty check that I got, which was one third of the total royalty check, because Gary keeps two thirds and I got one third, I bought a new car. And that proved to me that, well, by God, the best, and he, by the way, he never threw, he never threw another piece at me or did that again. I think he was making a point, he was being theatrical, but I realized that the point he was making and the theatricality of it needed to be done. And I have been doing that with myself ever since. And I deal with people who come to me for critiques, brutal, theatrical, if I need to be, and all niceties put aside, eagle left at the door, let's get the job done. And that was, that was just a priceless lesson to learn. You know, I've, I've, I've always been the opposite of Gary. I guess I was raised as a Methodist minister's son. And I was always taught that other other people's feelings were far more important than my own. And so it's been very difficult for me throughout my career to be brutally honest on crits of other copywriters work. But I had the opportunity to work on a promotion with Dan Rosenthal a few years back. And I sent him a rough outline of the offer. It wasn't even sales copy, it was just, I want to get, I want to be clear on the offer here, what are we providing for the money? I think it was a $5,000 investment service that we were selling into a house file. And so without, you know, with, with, with all of the disclaimer, this isn't copy, I just want to make sure I got the offer right. He wrote back and he says, this is of course limp dick copy. So even though it wasn't copy, and he knew it wasn't copy, he couldn't resist the opportunity to try to leave his scar. And I've made that throughout my career, you know, working with Martin Weiss has been interesting because he's from the Rosenthal School too. But he's a nice guy. And he doesn't get, you know, in your face like, like Rosenthal does. But the number of drafts that we did on a lot of those Weiss pieces was truly astounding. Right, you get to you can get to the same point. And I'm not, I'm not, you know, I, I, I don't try to insult people, which, which other mentors I've worked with have not been so nice about. But so, so I don't could gratuitously try to crush their spirit. And I'm very aware not to crush their spirit. So I always throw a few things in there to try to help them. And I try to find something positive in there too. But sometimes the best thing, you know, with one of the hardest things as, as I'm sure you're, you're, you're finding out working, working with all these co-writers, one of the hardest thing for a rookie writer to do is a throwaway what he wrote. Yeah, one of the first things a pro does when he realizes at that, at that ah-ha experience that even if he's worked for two weeks on a piece of copy, and it ain't work, it goes in the trash. Yeah. So telling, telling a rookie writer in no uncertain terms, and he's a trash, everything that his precious baby just ain't going to make it in the real world. That's a hard lesson for them to learn. No matter how many times you tell them that professionals do this, the first time they do it, even the first few times they do it, it's just hard that they don't want to do it. They don't want to get rid of that stuff. They spent too much time on it. This was a creation. It reminds me, I'm a, I'm an art fan. I buy local art from local artists, and occasionally a national artist. And, you know, so I wind up looking at a lot of art in scene step. And I have a complaint with the art world in that, you know, Picasso was great as he was. He ruined it for a lot of people. Picasso was a great artist who then decided he was going to go off into another realm. And that's when he kind of invented, you know, cubism and abstract art. What happened after that, though, ruined art for a lot, for in a lot of ways, because people started with what he ended with thinking, I can do that. They started with that, didn't learn their chops, didn't learn the craft, don't understand how to mix paint or do colors or don't understand the craft of the, of using the brush and the palette or anything and start doing their own stuff. And they think that everything they do has to be brilliant because, quote unquote, they're an artist. And, you know, that's kind of ruined things, you know, for me in a lot, in a lot of ways. So, but then I, you know, I, I, I'm always looking for that artist who has defied that and really learns his craft. But that's my, my, my first lesson that I want to drive through to people that that I teach how to write copy is learn the craft. It won't stop you from being a genius later. You know, it's not going to hurt your, your brilliance to just pause, learn the craft, read the elements of style by E. B. White of, you know, spend some time going over copy and over it and over it and over it and trashing it if you need to. You know, I, I write fiction too, Clayton, and I just trashed 200 pages of fiction novel. I just tossed it. I sat back and I had to have a little of, you know, a little meeting of the minds with myself. And I said, I can't fix it. I, this is, you know, I made a mistake early in the piece and I went the wrong direction and it needs to go. And I let it go and it was the relief. And it wouldn't, and I could have stuck with that thing for the next 10 years struggling to make it right rather than trash it, start fresh and I'll be where I need to be in a few months. That's really great advice. That's really great advice. Some, some of the actually better writers I've worked with over the years don't seem to have that little angel on their shoulder that says, you know, this really sucks. And then, and then the guts to just trash it. And as a result, even though they're great writers and great salespeople, their track record is inconsistent. They don't, they, they tend to go from, you know, out of the park grand slams to, to loud horrible losers to another out of the park grand slam. And I really think that ability to, to objectively analyze your copy and then having the balls to throw it away when it's not right is what it takes. In Freudian psychology, and I throw, I toss this stuff in because I have a BA in psychology, it's the only time I get to use it. In Freudian psychology, it's called the super ego. And in, in psychology, when, when you're talking with a therapist, the super ego is that voice inside your head that is your conscience, essentially, but often it's, it's a negative thing. It's often the voice of a parent, you know, telling you you're no good or telling you you're not trying hard enough or whatever. So it's often when you go into therapy, that's the one you try to fix me. And, and if, if, if I'm understanding you, you did the same thing. I installed a very critical super ego inside of me based on my work with Howard, with Abraham, with the, with the, with the cruel dictator that I of the version of myself is in there. It's this big amalgam of all the, all the influences that I've had in my life sitting there. And I let that little, that little monster loose on my copy. And, you know, I often, you know, I, I don't want to hear this stuff, you know, I, I, I don't want to hear that I'm going to have to trash what I just spent two weeks on. But I want that voice and I have cultivated it. And once you, once you have that breakthrough of letting that voice come through, you know, you're just off to the races because you have the governor that most writers don't have, you have the, it's a safety valve as, as, as you were alluding to, you know, when, you know, if you write a great piece, a lot of times you can go off half cock thinking anything I write is going to be great because I, you know, I just wrote a home run for, you know, Gore or whatever. And it's just not true. You're only as good as your last piece or actually your last sentence, really. I like that. Another thing that, that I hear coming from you is something I've been discussing with my, the people who copy Chief Cubs with me. And that's this whole theme of creativity within a discipline. So many of the writers that I work with see this craft as being primarily a creative one. And when you go back through their pieces, you'll see a certain lack of discipline going through. Yes. And if there are four or five sales that they need to make in order to close the final sale, they wind up making two or three. Right. They might, and leaving a lot of money on the table. Yeah. Clayton, this is, this is a subject near and dear to my heart. I grew up without discipline, more or less. I was a, I was the baby of the family. So my, you know, my parents, you know, made all the mistakes with the first two kids. And then me was kind of lay safe, fair, you know, just, just let, let the kid, you know, go. And a lot of that was great. I had free reign of a wonderful area that I grew up in and got to roam around and do a lot of things. And I was kind of unfettered. The downside was I never really learned to discipline myself. And I was a bad college student. I wound up getting fired from a lot of jobs because I just, I didn't understand how to sit down, figure out what the job required, and then discipline myself to do that. And it took me a long time. I was 32 before I finally went out as a freelancer. And I took a simple vow. I sat down myself. And for what, for one thing, for every business book I read, or book on marketing or sales or, or copyright for every one of those, I also read a self help book, like I read all the Augman Dino books, I read Dale Carnegie's books. And so I was trying to balance out that site. And I made a simple vow when I went out as a freelancer. And it's, it's, it's a cliche, but it helped me immensely. And it was business before pleasure. And before that, in my life, it had been pleasure, and then business at this time. You know, so there was a lot of partying, and there was a lot of unfocused behavior, because, you know, if the choice was go somewhere and do a job or find the best party going on, I'd, I'd go to the party. And I decided that, you know, business before pleasure didn't mean that I didn't have any pleasure or fun in my life. What it meant was, I got the job done first. And then unfettered, I would be able to go and have as much fun. And that led to, you know, work hard, play hard. What that took was an amazing amount of discipline for an undisciplined guy. And I found out that, like, like people who get into martial arts find out, discipline can be a wonderful thing. It's, it frees you. It's not, it's not the jail cell that a lot of people think it might be, where, you know, they, they look at, at people who seem to be disciplined, and they shudder thinking that it's not a life I want to lead because they're doing things I don't want to do. You have to work it into, into your own life. But discipline, you know, goes to that, that thing of learning the craft first. You know, discipline yourself to understand what, what you're doing. And you know, that whole talk about creativity just makes me shudder because, because I would, when I was down in LA, I hung out with a lot of what I call the D list in Hollywood. You know, the, the A list would be like Jack Nicholson and, and top stars. The D list was full of failed producers and, and screenwriters who had never sold, you know, a line of copy and, and actresses who were really professional waitresses and things like that. It was, it was a fun group to be with. Actually, Quentin Tarantino was part of that crowd. And he actually burst out of it. And there were a few others that, you know, burst out. But I learned that the problem with the ones that weren't going to burst out weren't ever going to burst out where it was just a lack of, of discipline. They called themselves a screenwriter, but they had one screenplay they'd done and it needed editing. And they were just hauling that around. And they thought the whole thing was getting a meeting with John Candy's people. They'd be really happy about that or getting an option from Paramount, which, which an option just meant it, you know, they put the, they didn't want to do it, but they didn't want anybody else to do it either. So they put it on a shelf for 50 grand or something. And, you know, you wouldn't, you'd never get the movie made. And it was like, I, I, and I really didn't want to get into that Hollywood scene. But I remember thinking if I did, I would be spending all my time writing and figuring out the craft of this thing, of what worked and I would have 50 screenplays to haul around, rather than one and, you know, and try to try to make that be my, my one thing going on. And it was the same with sales. I didn't want to be a one trick pony. So I just really tried to make my bag of tricks as deep and varied as possible. And it's, it's, it's, if you love the business, if this is the niche for you, copywriting or advertising or even marketing, you know, none of this is onerous. It's all very much fun. When I find a piece that works, when I stumble upon a, a bent of being a piece or a Clayton Makepiece piece or a piece that I know is a control, regardless of who wrote it, you know, I'm in heaven because I know I'm, you know, this is something that's, you know, I'm going to find something in there that I can use later, or it's going to remind me of something I've forgotten about. And it's a, it's a lifetime of learning, you know, it's just, you have to recognize the lessons, take the lessons to heart, realize you're probably going to violate them at some point, relearn them, relearn them again if you have to, but just stay in it because it's a, you know, it's a never ending game and it's fun. If it's not fun, you're in the wrong business. And if it is fun, then, you know, you get to sit back and once while and say, you know, I'm working at home, I'm, I'm, I'm in my pajamas, I'm the dog sleeping at my feet, you know, yeah, that's, this job's a little hard, and I'm not finding the, the right sales angle that I want, but I'll get to it eventually because I'm, you know, applying the right, the right principles to this whole job. But still, you know, though it's uncomfortable right now, I'm still having the time of my life because I'm in the right job. You know, I'm, I'm, I'm doing, I'm doing something I love, I'm, I'm making money writing, getting that, I don't know about you, but get, for me, getting that first check for writing something was just a revelatory moment. It was, it was, it was just an amazing moment in my life. I thought, you know, I remember staring at the check for a very long time, it was $500 I got for writing a piece for, you know, some, some local person. And it just, I just thought, this is what I want to do for the rest of my life. And it was very, very clear to me that that's what I wanted to do. Yeah, I remember that feeling and looking at that check and realizing that I'd earned every penny of it. Right. There was no employer who was obligated to pay me a salary. Now this was something that I had earned. And it got especially good when royalties started kicking in back in the 80s. And you knew you were getting, in essence, a percentage of the sales that you produced. And I also remember Clayton, the moment when I got a check, I think it was from Rodale, I thought, I have no idea what this check is from. And I thought about for a minute, I thought, I don't care. And I cashed it. I thought the bookkeeper figured out later. And I remember that being a seminal moment. That was just a moment where I realized, okay, you know, if there is a point of a righty, I've arrived, I'm not sure where I'm at, I'm going to I'm not going to stop, you know, trying, I'm not going to rest on my laurels. But that was just the point where, you know, just, okay, I kind of maybe I got this a little more figured out than I was, you know, that I thought. So, so, Joe, when you see other writers work out there today, what are the top two or three crits that you have of the stuff you're seeing in the mail and on the net right now? I'm sorry, the top two or three, what? Critiques, criticism, problems that I have with with the copy. I'd say the first thing is that, which actually is a segue from what you were talking about last time, it's what I call being a sales detective. It's getting, when you're dealing with a client, you got to get past the company line. Usually as a freelancer, when you're brought in, you know, you wouldn't be brought in if they had stuff that was working. If the owner of the company was writing or his secretary was writing or his partner was writing, if somebody was writing stuff that was working, they wouldn't need you. So you're usually being brought in in some point of crisis. It might be early in the crisis, it might be late in the crisis, but something ain't working and you're there to fix it. And if you just take the company line, if you take the materials they give you and listen to the boss talk about his business and who his customers are and what he wants to do, if that's where you stop, you're not going to be able, you're not going to have the ammo to write what needs to be written, which is to break out of that company line that isn't working for them. And that's why you're there. So that's why I talk about doing things like going beneath, going behind the back of the boss. And I say that figuratively, although you do it in, you know, literally if you have to, but talk to the secretary and find out what the gossip is about the company, go talk to the feet in the street, the guys are actually selling this, whether it's face to face, or selling it on the phone or whatever, get the stories they tell about what's going on about the customers. Often, the guy who owns the company and the guy you're dealing with, and by the way, I only recommend to freelancers that you deal with the guy who's going to sign the check. If you're dealing with somebody who can't sign the check, he's going to have to take whatever you say and do and take it to another level that you're not going to have any influence with, and it's going to ruin what you do. So I don't know if that's been your policy or not. But so, excuse me, so you want to deal with the guy who's signed the check, but then you want to go behind his back and find out what the gossip is, what the stories are about the product, talk to the guys that created the thing, and talk to customers and get the story behind the testimonials. Often, the client won't have good testimonials. And this is true even with the big mailers. I'm only astonished when they don't have a lot of these big mailers don't have a separate department doing nothing but going after great testimonials. They take letters, they get sent to them or emails, and they kind of publish them whole, and they don't really teach people how to write testimonials. And that's a whole art in and of itself. Being specific, not doing just the raw, raw kind where this product's great, I really like it. When if you talk to that person, you'd find out that it quadrupled his income in 48 hours, or it saved his marriage, or it did things that would become headlines. And what you're doing there is you're looking for hooks, you're looking for the story behind the story. And often when I find the hook, such as the one that gets tossed around the most is the one-legged golfer, that was a story that the guy didn't want to tell anymore because he thought everybody would be bored with it. And he was trying to sell an instructional video on golf, and he had his revelation golfing behind a group of people who included a one-legged golfer who was just whacking the heck out of the ball. And this guy realized that two-legged golfers could use the same balance secret this one-legged guy was using, and that's how he constructed this special swing. But he never told anybody about it. And it took me 45 minutes of talking to him on the phone before he finally told the story. He didn't think it was very important. As soon as I heard it, I thought that's the hook. And that became the headline, and that ad ran for 10 years, blah, blah, blah. So that would be the first mistake. The second mistake would be not having that mental conversation with a skeptic. I think you've talked about the classic line, Walk a Mile in the reader's shoes, in the client's shoes. You want to be able to get out of yourself, get away from the client, and read your piece as a skeptical reader would read it. A lot of people get caught up, they fall in love with their product, and they think their product is the product. I always tell them, your product ain't the product. Your product is the vehicle for what your real product is, and what your real product is, is the benefits you deliver. So you're not really selling a seminar. You're not selling a course or a book or even your consultancy. You're selling a better life for the reader. You're selling more money for the reader. You're selling some benefit for that. So you've got to get straight on that. So don't start out selling the seminar. You've got to grease them up a little bit first. Get them excited about what will happen. The seminar is merely the vehicle for the information that you have chosen. It could have been something else. It could have been a face-to-face chat on the phone, or excuse me, it could have been a chat on the phone. It could have been a course that you wrote out. You chose a seminar. So the product that you think is the product is not really the product. And when I write to a particular market, I like to think of my potential reader as this large, senambulent blob welded to the couch that wouldn't get up to save his life if the house was burning down. And I have to get him to move to get so motivated to light a fire so hot under his butt that he's going to take out his wallet, give a credit card number over the phone, or tap it on online to strangers he doesn't know for a product he can't see, have smell or feel, and want it bad enough to be able to take money that was his and give it to me. And that is one of the hardest things you can do in human interaction. So don't take that part lightly. And that's what that's the biggest, that's one of the biggest mistakes I see people doing. And the third one would be the classy gun to the head thing. When early in my career as a freelancer, in fact, during my first jobs, I remember sitting down thinking, wow, I didn't have the confidence, I didn't have any winners really under my belt, I was literally beginning. I remember sitting down and thinking, I'm not sure if I can do this or not, but if I am going to do it, and you know, if I can't do it, I don't have a plan B. I mean, the business world had been spitting me out regularly for my entire career. You know, I don't like ties. I don't like the feeling of a tie cinched up against my neck. I don't like getting up in the morning. I know that you get up early, Clayton, but for me, that's just, that's anathema. I generally work late, I would like to work my own hours. I like to work on my own schedule. And that's what freelancing provided for me. But if I didn't make it work by writing winning pieces, I didn't know what was going to happen. So that feeling was of having a gun to my head that was going to go off if I didn't make this piece work. Well, rather than freak me out, this freed me, this freed me of all anxiety because I would ask myself, with a gun to my head, would I write this headline? Would I use that word in the headline or would I want a stronger word? Would I open it this way? Would I construct the offer this way? How does the letter look? Would I, you know, with a gun to my head, would I experiment? And the answer almost always was no. You go with what works. You start looking at the classic stuff. So that's when I really start hauling out John Caple's books again and looking at how he might approach it. You know, rereading Claude Hopkins on a regular basis, finding examples of other successful mailings or letters or ads in the same genre, you know, that and see how they handle it. You know, see how things. So it's just, you know, don't do the guesswork. And that gets back to your question about your or talking about creativity, you know, with a gun to your head, would you get creative and work on a theory? You know, and my answer was always no, I want to do what's proven, what what has already been proven to work, what and increase my chances to make this work with the gun to the head. And, you know, a lot of rookies don't understand that. The best rookies I've had, the ones that have advanced the fastest, do have a gun to their head. They don't have a plan B, they need to make this work. And that, you know, it can be a little scary at first, but that's the motivation you need. I'm constantly struck by how similar the themes are between writers who have done well. For me, the gun to my head was a wife and two kids, right, all expecting me to provide a living. And Clayton, probably not being totally supportive. I mean, maybe even being a little skeptical, right? Highly skeptical. Right. Yeah, the wife was from a very wealthy family. And here I was saying, I'm not going to get another job. I'm going to make this work. Yeah, it was a very big gun to the head. What are the biggest mistakes in your experience that clients make when working with copywriters? You've covered several, expecting us to take dictation, you know, hiring us and then essentially writing the copy themselves. Right. What are the things that you look for clients to provide to help you write better copy? I came across the perfect client in the late 80s, early 90s, they are still clients of mine. And what they did, excuse me, they, they had actually worked briefly with Jay Abraham, and then worked with Gary Halbert. And that's how they found me. And they they actually stalked me. They begged me to work with them. I wasn't taking clients at the time. I finally took them on. And what they did as part of the deal to get me to work for them, because the money wasn't doing it, I didn't, I didn't want their money. I didn't want the royalty thing. Nothing was doing it. And finally, they made it very clear to me that they would take the leash off. And by taking the leash off, that's, that's everything that you just mentioned, the censorship, the preconceived, you know, nonsense that clients had being a scaredy cat about mailing stuff. All of that would be gone. They would mail everything I wrote as I wrote it. And I didn't have to think very long about that. Because I thought, this can be my laboratory. You know, because even with the big mailers, you know, they would change it as, as you know, even with guys who know, and sometimes when you're working with somebody who knows direct response, maybe is an expert in direct response and you're writing for them, such as a lot of clients, I know that that's you've you've had in the past. You know, they're going to want to put their two cents in, or you know, the thing will morph a little bit. This was an opportunity for me to have absolutely no input at all beyond the input I wanted, which was the information I'd have them get testimonials. I would tell them what testimonials I wanted. I said, I want these six testimonials. Don't stop, put somebody on it. Don't stop until you find six people who will say approximately what I want you to say here. And usually I would, I would have those testimonials cover three, four, five, six of the major benefit points. So I didn't want six testimonials all saying the same thing. I wanted specific testimonials covering the points that I was trying to make in the in the in the pitch. I would, you know, and often I would get involved before the product was created, because I would I would put the nicks on certain products. I'd say, Well, you can do that, but I'm not going to write for it because it's a loser. And I'll tell you why. And I talked to them then would and I helped them work it out so that the product would actually have some ammo. Because as as as all writers know, bad copy can't sell free gold, but good copy can sell a non existent product. That's why a lot of, you know, crooked direct marketers go to jail. They, you know, the copy, you know, copy really is king. You, you know, you can make a case, you know, if you have good ammo. And of course, the unscrupulous people in our in our profession who have, you know, given direct marketing a black eye over the years, you know, are crooks. They just go out and say what they know the person needs to hear. But there's a lesson there too, for the people who are scrupulous and have ethics. And that is, that is, you need ammo, you need to have the stuff to be able to create the sales pitch that gets that, you know, that gets that blob to move. And so these, these kinds of it for a long time just mail everything that I write as as I've written it. And it allowed me to expand a little bit because I would tell them sometimes I'm experiment a little bit, I put something in the headline, I've changed the offer of that I've done this. In my experience, everything that every fiber of my body tells me this should work. But I'm telling you guys, I'm experimenting a little bit, and they would never even question it. They just say fine, and they'd mail it. And I've been lucky. Well, luck has a little bit to do with it. But a lot of it is, I've, you know, the stuff has just worked consistently. I've had very few bad experiences with it with the things I've mailed that have mailed or gone online with them. And this is over. Actually, I say 10 years, it's been been 15 or 17 years I worked with this client. So that that that was just huge for me to have them take the leash off me and just just say do what you know will work. And and we'll back you up on it. And that is such a rare quality in a client. So tell me what's the smartest thing you've done to increase your income as a copywriter? Oh, the same thing everybody else did. I learned about royalties and commissions. To get a piece of your own products, rather than your own teaching products. Well, that that's a whole separate thing. I that I am my own worst client and my best client, because of course I will, I don't run my copy in front of anybody else except myself. So I get to write what I want to write about myself. I've done a couple of seminars, I get to do the seminar that I think should be done. And and I've been rewarded by by this, you know, I get to I get to critique pieces the way I do. I mean, nobody's telling me how to do this, except the marketplace. It turns out that the way I want to teach that I think works the best is being received extremely well in the marketplace. And I've got the you know, I've got the people out there, you know, earning six figures and above to prove it. And you know, I've got a big drawer bursting with the testimonials here. People are taking one word ideas and changing one word in their headline and doubling response. They're changing one minor thing. And often they're changing major things. And it just it's just revolutionizing their their their business. And this is not a mystery to you, to you or me or anybody else, because all I'm doing is the stuff we've been talking about, you know, I'm helping people to get in touch with what works, because, you know, people are getting into business faster than ever before with with the web. You can be in business tonight from scratch, really, especially with downloadable books, you don't have to worry about printing. You know, you can almost get there faster than you can think. And if you're not already well versed in how to create a good sales pitch, you know, you're going to make a lot of mistakes. For a long time, you could make a lot of mistakes on the web and still make it work because there weren't, you know, there wasn't a lot of competition. Well, that has changed. And anybody that's got anything that's working can expect a flood of competition to come, you know, down on their head very, very quickly. So the person who's going to win is the person who knows how to create the good sales pitch who understands classic salesmanship. That's a skill that will never leave you no matter what market you're in or what you're doing. It's it's it's it's the basis of everything. As I say at my seminars, nothing happens until the copy gets written. Yeah, I'm seeing I think we're all seeing on the web now the increased cost of driving people to landing pages and websites combined with deliverability problems, combined with dropping response rates because of competition is making copy more important than ever on the web. That's right. And it will continue to do so. However, the secret that you know, a lot of people is, you know, you know, as as General Motors comes on to the web as as General Foods and Edison and all these major companies start coming on to the web, they're going to bring their own preconceived ideas about what copies should be, which is pretty much what Madison Avenue has been talking about. So your secret weapon, and what's great about the web is you can be a one man band working out of your garage, you know, with a little product, you can still take on the big guys, because the because of the nature of the web. And in fact, you can beat them because you can use copy secrets and salesmanship secrets that they won't allow themselves to use or don't don't even realize, you know, they're used to working on volume and and owning the retail stores. It's like, you know, General Foods pays for shelf position for Cheerios so that it's eye level as you walk into the store, where you can't do that on the web. Or you can't do it, you know, the the equivalent of it any better than anyone else can do, especially with the way Google has democratized things by keeping everything fairly, fairly, you know, if you're clever enough, if you understand what to do, you won't pay a lot for Google AdWords still, although that all this may change tomorrow, you know, as as as, you know, if the US government decided they wanted to regulate the web, that would be the end of a lot of the gold rush going on. But no matter what happens, no matter what kind of restrictions get slapped on, no matter what, what kind of competition comes on, no matter what else happens, knowing how to construct a classic sales pitch is is going to keep you in, in, you know, in, in, in wealth and in, in freedom for as long as you as you care to partake, because that's the secret that none of your competitors are going to beat you on if you become an expert at it. You know, I've often said that if the web costs $500, a thousand, 90% of the web marketers would be out of business. Because because they're so sloppy. Yeah. Yeah. Because the copy's so weak, they made it on the basis of the of the free medium, not on the quality of their offers and their copy. And absolutely right. They're using copy that we put in any direct mail market or out of business in no time flat. Right. And I just see huge opportunities for writers going forward as the web becomes more expensive and more competitive. And, and it needs to be said, right now, the web has forced the literacy rate in America to start to go up, even though it's, you know, it's never going to rock the the rest of the educated world. But, you know, people are now writing, my father, who is 84 years old, wrote his first email. He capped it out with two fingers. It was like 12 words long and there was two misspellings and it wasn't a complete sentence. That was his first email. Now he's writing me pages of stuff. He's become fluent again. And, and, and on the web, the copy, the written word is still king, although even if the web changes and replaces television and somehow video becomes much more common and things, copy will still be king because even the video won't be done until the copy is written. Even if you're making it up as you go standing in front of a camera that's going to be broadcast, you know, on the web, you have to create the sales pitch. You have to, you have to do that. And whether you write it down or not, you are constructing copy in your head or you're working off of a script or whatever. So, yeah, the, the, the act of being able to write down a classic sales pitch will never, ever go away, no matter what happens to civilization or technology. That's great, John. Well, we've gone through our list of questions here. I was wondering if you have any, anything you'd like to leave us with? Anything we haven't covered? No, I think this has been one of the better interviews. I always enjoy talking with, you know, guys who really understand. I get a lot of requests for interviews and I do them. And there is a standard list of questions that people tend to ask, which is more or less from a rookie standpoint. So getting this kind of stuff, which goes a lot deeper, is very refreshing. And, you know, I could, I could go on for hours and you and I could tell war stories, you know, for, for hours too, because, you know, from our similar past and also from the different experiences we've had. So it's, you know, it's, it's a joy talking to somebody who, who not just understands it, but it of course is a master at the craft. So it's, it's the idea at the Algonquin table, you know, writing is still a very lonely profession. And email has helped. I mean, I have email correspondence going on all the time with people and I waste probably more time than I should just contacting other writers and I'll spend time on the phone. But, you know, we, we, as, as, as you said, it is amazing to see the similarities in, in, you know, in, in writers, especially ones who have become successful. You know, how we took the same paths, yet we weren't taking notes and we weren't, we weren't, it wasn't that we didn't want to help each other. It's that it's, it's basically a lonely profession. So I think the fact that independently so many writers took the same path, however circuitously they got to that path is, is an important lesson in and of itself. So I think one of the things, you know, the, in interviewing Gary and Arthur Johnson and so many others, it all starts with capels and Reeves and Ogilvy and Claude and Claude Hopkins and, and then moves on to studying. You know, other people's writings. Right. And, but something that you said at the end of your bio too, the people fascinate you. Oh, yeah. Now it's just studying and I think you're, you know, you, you make fun of your, your BS in psychology. It's a B.A. actually. Did I put BS? Yeah, it should be B.A. there. Well, you make fun of it, but I mean, it predisposes you to being a student of human nature, which is to me one of the most important keys of selling. And, you know, I love going to the mall and just sitting there watching people interact with each other. Yeah. You know, it's funny you mentioned the psychology degree. Actually, maybe that helped me a little bit and I learned a little bit about demographics and things, but really where I learned it the most was guys like Halbert who never went to college, you know, using it on a street level. And that's when it just that a hot thing came from it. That's what was missing in my high fluten college education at the University of California with the lack of street level viability for this information. How did it really play out in the real world? And that's where advertised. That's what advertising is all about is real world application of, you know, high minded ideas, especially sociology and psychology and demographics. And if you love people, I'm kind of an introvert. I'm not sure if you are Clayton or not, but you know, I have to, parties sat me of energy more or less at hanging around people. I'm very much a classic introvert, although I do get out once in a while. But I still love people and I'm never bored with the way people act and talk and do things. And the fact that most of them are asleep or half asleep walking around in a zombie state has always been the case. The few who wake up are the ones who rise to success in this world. And one of my goals is to help people wake up, is to help more people wake up. And it's just ironic that advertising is one of the ways you can do it. You don't have to lull people into a trance in order to sell them, especially the way that you and I approach it. Often it's waking them up to what's going on that you help. So you're performing not just a public good, you're performing a good on the visceral level, individual by individual, because if you have a great product, you can really start to change the world on a kind of a small basis. Our culture and the whole western world is an engine driven by advertising. And it doesn't have to be that vapid stuff. The stuff we write is full of our life experiences, we tell stories. The whole storytelling culture was starting to die in the western world until long copies started reviving it again. We thrive on long stories. We're all storytellers, or bullshitters really, but in a good way, in a way that brings us back to that community level where I have some information I'd like to share with you. Would you like to know it? It's probably a secret to you right now. Yes, I would. I'd very much like to know what you're talking about if it will get me from where I'm at to where I want to be. It's great stuff, John. I think we can go on for hours. In fact, we're going to have to one of these days get together over drinks. Yeah, I'd love to do it.
